title: The rise of the agentic customer: the trillion-dollar shift defining a new era of competition
full_text: Other Editions 06 May 2026 Business The rise of the agentic customer: the trillion-dollar shift defining a new era of competition theaustralian.com.au Brad Milliken 5 May 2026 04:17 GMT 1011 words English AUSTOL © News Pty Limited. No redistribution is permitted. For decades, executives have asked the same question: how do we win, serve and retain the customer? The answer is about to be rewritten, because the customer of the near future may not be human. New economic actors are emerging in Australian markets: agentic customers. These AI agents act on a consumer’s behalf, and while boardrooms focus on deploying agentic AI inside organisations, the bigger shift is in the consumer’s pocket. Agents acting for customers will matter more to an organisation’s future than those operating inside it. They will redefine what a customer is and could redirect a trillion dollars of consumer spending in our economy each year. They have near-infinite time and attention, are immune to complexity and choice fatigue, and are driven by objective logic rather than emotion or brand affinity. They are the most powerful and ruthless customer organisations will ever have to win. They will compare more products and promotions than humans can; switching products, providers or plans weekly, daily or the moment a better offer appears. They will re-bundle propositions on the customer’s own terms and detect service failures instantly, escalating complaints or claims the second eligibility is triggered. As agents co-ordinate with one another, they will pool demand to turn individual buying power into collective leverage. This is no longer hypothetical. Our 2026 survey of 3,000 Australian consumers found 73 per cent have used AI to research products, with 64 per cent using AI at least weekly. Half would trust an agent to search, compare and transact for them, and 79 per cent would use an agent to save money. Adoption is here and accelerating. What it means for Australian consumers Smarter choices and greater buying power are only the beginning. As agentic customers pool demand, the economics of entire markets shift in the consumer’s favour. The greatest gains will come in grudge purchases, including superannuation, energy, telecommunications, insurance and banking, where inertia has long underwritten margins. Agents end that inertia, holding an active market persona around the clock. For a 25-year-old, the compounded savings from switching providers, when reinvested over a working life, could mean hundreds of thousands of dollars more at retirement. What it means for organisations Agentic customers do not care about an organisation’s brand campaign, SEO budget, loyalty program or website. These are new types of customers the whole organisation must pivot around, requiring a rethink of sales, marketing, service and the operating model. If an organisation’s competitive advantage cannot be read by a machine, it simply won’t exist. The opportunities are real. Agentic customers will help businesses sell more niche products by surfacing the long tail rather than just the bestsellers. They will also uncover discounts humans miss, lifting conversion rates. The risks are equally tangible. Revenue is at stake from elevated churn while claims and complaint volumes may also rise. Retention costs will also climb and bundled propositions, long a profit lever, face exposure as agents disassemble them, strip optional add-ons and force pricing transparency on every component. Our research points to where agentic customers will land first: with the 25-to-49-year-old cohort, at the ‘compare options’ stage of the customer journey. If organisations do not have a strong agentic customer proposition at this point, they are already losing revenue they cannot see. So, how can organisations win in the age of agentic customers? • Understand the new customer. Develop a clear view of how agentic customers behave and the decision-making logic they apply. Winning a single agentic customer is different to winning a collective of agents acting for a household or a buying coalition, and the go-to-market strategy for each is not the same. • Design propositions to be machine-readable. Outcomes, experiences and offers must be continuously optimised and agent ready. Differentiation that resonates only with humans will not survive contact with an algorithm. • Rethink channels. Identify which channels best engage agents and what that means for those built for humans today. • Redesign the operating model. The front office and wider organisation must be reorganised around agentic customers. A key decision will be whether these capabilities sit centrally, are distributed across teams or housed in a dedicated unit. • Reassess value capture. The lifetime value of agentic customers will differ sharply from that of human customers, as they are likely to pay lower price points, generate more claims and reduce margins on bundles. That forces organisations to rethink which types of customers they want to attract. The rise of agentic customers is the defining economic opportunity and risk of our time, and the window to respond is closing. The organisations that own the next era of competition will be those that recognise the customer they are building for is no longer the one in front of them. It is the customer that evaluates them a thousand times a day in silence. Brad Milliken is Partner and Head of Deloitte Digital. summary: The emergence of 'agentic customers', represented by AI agents acting on behalf of consumers, is set to transform Australian markets by enhancing consumer power and redefining customer engagement. These AI agents will facilitate smarter purchasing decisions and challenge traditional business models, particularly in sectors like superannuation and insurance. Companies must adapt their strategies to cater to these new customers or risk losing revenue and competitive advantage.
publication:
Aussies reject AI – but it’s not for the reason you think
theaustralian.com.au04 May 2026 20:30 GMT783 wordsJared Lynch

Chief executives of Australia’s biggest companies and Anthony Albanese want us all to use more artificial intelligence-powered tools – but there is a big problem.
heading: Data Science and AI
key_points: Agentic customers will significantly influence consumer spending and market dynamics.
Businesses need to redesign their propositions and operating models to accommodate machine-readable offers.
Failure to adapt to the needs of agentic customers could lead to increased churn and reduced profitability.
relevance: Understanding the rise of agentic customers is crucial for actuaries and financial services professionals as it impacts customer engagement strategies and market competitiveness.
